content.content:
content: '# Web Document


  ## Metadata


  - title: Overdraft Services | Chase.com

  - url: https://www.chase.com/personal/checking/overdraft-services

  - source: Chase

  - domain: chase.com


  ## Content


  # Chase Overdraft Services


  Chase describes its overdraft services as information customers should know about
  overdrafts. The page explains that some overdraft features are included with most
  checking accounts and do not require enrollment, while other overdraft-related services
  are optional.


  ## Standard Overdraft Practice


  Under Standard Overdraft Practice, when there is not enough money in a checking
  account to cover a transaction, Chase may cover certain transactions for a fee at
  its discretion. The decision may depend on account history, deposits, and the transaction
  amount.


  The covered transaction types described on this page include items such as checks,
  recurring debit card transactions, and automatic payments. The page states that
  the standard cost is a $34 Overdraft Fee per transaction, with a maximum of three
  Overdraft Fees per business day, for a total of $102. Transactions of $5 or less
  do not receive an Overdraft Fee.


  ## Chase Overdraft Assist


  Chase Overdraft Assist is included in Standard Overdraft Practice and provides extra
  time to help avoid a $34 Overdraft Fee. Chase states that if the account is overdrawn
  by $50 or less at the end of the business day, there is no overdraft fee. If the
  account is overdrawn by more than $50 at the end of the business day, the customer
  may avoid an overdraft fee by making a deposit or transfer so the account is overdrawn
  by $50 or less before 11 p.m. Eastern Time, or 8 p.m. Pacific Time, on the next
  business day.


  ## Optional overdraft services


  The page also describes optional services that may help avoid potential overdraft
  fees. Overdraft Protection lets a customer link a Chase savings account as a backup
  to a checking account. If enough money is available in the linked savings account,
  Chase transfers the exact amount needed to cover an overdraft transaction. Chase
  states that there is no cost to enroll and no fee to transfer money between Chase
  accounts.


  Chase Debit Card Coverage lets the customer choose how Chase handles everyday debit
  card transactions, such as groceries, gasoline, or dining out, when there is not
  enough money in the account. If coverage is off, the transaction will be declined
  and no Overdraft Fee will be charged. If coverage is on, Chase may pay the overdraft
  transaction at its discretion and charge the $34 Overdraft Fee.


  ## Digital tools


  Chase lists digital tools that help customers monitor and manage spending, including
  the Chase Mobile app, account alerts for low or overdrawn balances, and Spending
  Planner. The page also says customers can consider Chase Secure Banking, a checking
  account with no overdraft fees.

  '

content: '# Web Document


  ## Metadata


  - title: Chase Total Checking | Understanding checking and savings account fees
  | Chase.com

  - url: https://www.chase.com/personal/fees/total-checking

  - source: Chase

  - domain: chase.com


  ## Content


  # Understanding checking and savings account fees


  Chase provides fee information for Chase Total Checking and other checking and savings
  accounts. The Chase Total Checking section says customers should open the page details
  to see fees and ways to avoid certain fees.


  ## Chase Total Checking account fees


  The Monthly Service Fee for Chase Total Checking is $0 or $15. Chase lists several
  ways to have the monthly fee be $0 during each statement period. These include receiving
  at least $500 in qualifying electronic deposits, keeping a $1,500 balance at the
  beginning of each day, keeping a $5,000 average beginning day balance in the account
  or in combination with linked qualifying personal deposits or investments, or linking
  the account to a qualifying checking account. If none of the listed options are
  met, the monthly service fee is $15.


  ## Overdraft Fees


  The Chase Total Checking fee page describes an overdraft as a situation where the
  customer makes a purchase or payment, or writes a check, and there is not enough
  money in the checking account to cover it. If Chase pays the item, the fee is $34
  per transaction during nightly processing, beginning with the first transaction
  that overdraws the account balance by more than $50. Chase states that the maximum
  is three fees per business day, up to $102.


  The page also describes Chase Overdraft Assist. It helps customers with confidence
  and time: there are $0 overdraft fees if the account is overdrawn by $50 or less
  at the end of the business day, and there are $0 overdraft fees if the account is
  overdrawn by more than $50 and the customer brings the balance to overdrawn by $50
  or less by the end of the next business day.


  Chase also says it does not charge an overdraft fee for certain things, including
  transactions that are declined or returned unpaid and transactions of $5 or less.


  ## Other fee categories


  The page lists other Chase Total Checking fee categories, including ATM fees at
  Chase ATMs, ATM fees at non-Chase ATMs, wire transfer fees, and money order fees.
  For wire transfers, the fee varies depending on whether the wire is incoming or
  outgoing, domestic or international, and whether the transfer is completed with
  a banker, on chase.com, or through the Chase Mobile app.


  ## More details about fees


  For more details about fees, the page links to the Deposit Account Agreement and
  the Additional Banking Services and Fees PDF.

  '

content: '# Web Document


  ## Metadata


  - title: Chase Customer Service: We can help you! | Chase

  - url: https://www.chase.com/digital/customer-service

  - source: Chase

  - domain: chase.com


  ## Content


  # Chase Customer Service


  Chase Customer Service provides self-service actions, account-help categories, frequently
  asked questions, and contact options for customers who need banking support.


  ## Self-service actions


  Available self-service topics include making a payment, ordering checks, managing
  account alerts, replacing a lost or damaged card, disputing a charge, resetting
  a username or password, finding an account or routing number, reporting fraud, and
  getting payment support.


  ## Get help with your account


  Account-help categories include Credit Cards, Personal Banking, Investment & Retirement
  Planning, Chase Private Client, Auto Loans, Home Lending, Commercial Banking, and
  Business Banking.


  ## Frequently asked questions and phone support


  For Personal Banking, Chase lists Personal Banking Customer Service at 1-800-935-9935
  for questions about checking and savings accounts and debit cards. The page also
  lists separate customer-service numbers for credit cards, auto loans, and home lending.


  ## Still have questions


  Chase says customer help and technical support can assist with online and mobile
  banking support or account help. The page again lists Chase Customer Service at
  1-800-935-9935 and notes that operator relay calls are accepted. Chase Accessibility
  Services is also available.


  ## Social media and personal information


  Chase lists @ChaseSupport and messaging options on Facebook and Instagram. The page
  tells customers not to include account numbers or personal information in social-media
  messages.


  ## Schedule a meeting and feedback


  Customers can schedule a meeting with a banker to discuss an account or opening
  a new account. The page also provides a complaints and feedback option. Deposit
  products and related services are offered by JPMorgan Chase Bank, N.A., Member FDIC.

  '

Your account status shows a **posted $34 overdraft fee**. I’d avoid using an unfamiliar “quick refund” page unless it is clearly hosted on **chase.com** and reached through the Chase app or your logged-in Chase account. The search results I found included a third-party refund article, but not an official Chase “quick refund” form.

Here’s the safer approach:

1. **Check the transaction and fee details in the Chase app or chase.com.**
   - Confirm what caused the overdraft and whether the fee is actually labeled an overdraft fee.
   - Check whether the account qualified for **Overdraft Assist**—for example, if you were overdrawn by $50 or less at the end of the business day, or brought it down to $50 or less by the next business day. Chase says those situations should generally have a $0 overdraft fee.  
   Official details: [Chase Overdraft Services](https://www.chase.com/personal/checking/overdraft-services)

2. **If the fee appears incorrect, contact Chase through the app or the number on your debit card.**  
   Chase’s official personal-banking number is **1-800-935-9935**. Ask them to review the $34 fee and explain why it was charged. You can also visit a branch.

3. **If the fee is valid but unusual for you, request a courtesy review or one-time waiver.**  
   Don’t describe it as fraud unless you truly did not authorize the underlying transaction. A fee dispute and a transaction-fraud claim are different processes.

4. **Do not provide your login, password, one-time code, or full account number to a third-party page.**  
   Chase specifically advises against sharing personal information through unofficial channels.

Chase’s published standard is **$34 per qualifying overdraft transaction**, generally capped at three fees per business day, with no overdraft fee for certain declined/returned transactions or transactions of $5 or less. Because your fee is posted, the best next step is an official Chase review rather than the quick-refund page.
